results.english:
The same was true of Hanuman. It had never occurred to him to summon the Deity, yet Hanuman appeared in the form of Balaji,[footnote: Balaji — Hanuman, worshipped in Mehandipur, Rajasthan, and in the states of Madhya Pradesh, Haryana, and many other regions of northern India. He is considered the son of the wind, Vayu. His father carried him into his mother's lap at Mehandipur after the child (baladji) Hanuman tried to swallow the sun and Indra struck him with his thunderbolt sword. In northern India Balaji is revered as the ruler of the spirits.] the lord of spirits — and introduced him to the secrets of his realm. Perhaps initiation into the world of spirits was another part of the preparatory process for events yet to come? It seemed that all these forces and beings needed a mortal yogi who could enter the astral worlds by the power of consciousness and converse with phantoms without fear. In those worlds the human body was cumbersome, almost burdensome, and at the same time it was an anchor for a consciousness so powerful that even mighty astral beings acknowledged its outstanding qualities. A mortal capable of "walking" among immortal beings was too great a rarity to remain unnoticed.

…He was simply trying to walk the path of spiritual development, wanting only one thing — to find true yoga. Pursuing those aims, he came to know his own Spirit and strove to help it find a voice, to recognize its presence within the body, to find the eternal path. He was building a bridge of trust between the body and the one who animated it. Yoga was the method of knowing the soul's immortality, and every unfolding event and phenomenon supported his sadhana.

The progress and development of every teacher and disciple of this lineage was watched over, recognized. No matter how grandiose such claims might seem to skeptics, the facts were plain: Adi Shakti Herself had graced the smashan with Her presence, thereby confirming the spiritual merits of the Masters of Babaji's lineage.

Stone fragments — the breasts of Shakti

Child-actor in the guise of Shiva in the temple, 1998

Puja to Shiva

Puja to Shiva

Puja to Hanuman

Secrets of the Golden Bird

The Golden Bird

…He could not remember exactly when Hanuman had spoken of events that had changed the history of mankind. In particular, He mentioned two secrets of the Indian subcontinent.

In antiquity India had several names: Jambudvipa, or Bharatavarsha, the kingdom of Bharata, where the descendants of Bharata lived. The land was also called Sone kee chiriya — "the golden bird" — for its riches. Yet for some reason historians never noted that India had never been famous for its own reserves of precious metal. Where, then, did India's legendary gold come from? This is the first riddle, and the answer is hidden in the secrets of Indian Ayurvedic science, in the descriptions of producing alchemically processed mercury, which turned base metals into gold. To produce pure alchemical gold, siddhas and yogis used knowledge received from the sages, the rishis.

There was also another secret, which moved him even more. Indian mythology had always been regarded by Vedic scholars as the genuine history of the subcontinent. It was full of legends about saints who had performed austerities for centuries, about kings of antiquity who lived and ruled for hundreds and thousands of years. India was a land of immortals: the eight immortals, the chiranjivis, were to remain on Earth until the end of the current yuga; the nine immortal Nathas and the many rishis travelled across the subcontinent, teaching people and stimulating the development of societies, "churning" human history.

Modern science, of course, has never considered these myths credible. From the end of Vedic times the human lifespan shortened sharply, and legends of immortals were taken as entertaining tales. Yet the sage Valmiki mentions in the Ramayana that Lord Rama lived 11,000 years. Sri Krishna played the divine Lila for 125 years without showing the slightest sign of aging. The hunter's arrow that brought Krishna's undeniable longevity to an end was merely part of the divine plan. Had it not been for that circumstance, Krishna could have lived far longer.

The legendary emperor Vikramaditya ruled for 1,600 years; there were other sages, rishis, yogis, and kings who seem to have "cracked the code" of eternal life. Immortality was encoded in the very history of India, interwoven with the secrets of alchemical gold that grants eternal life. The whole science of alchemy was built around this aim; for a person possessed of enough gold, the desire to keep enjoying wealth was quite logical, for human nature itself is founded on an eternal chase after pleasures, and much can be bought with gold. But the aging body has no strength to taste the pleasures of the flesh; the taste for life fades over time and worldly temptations no longer impress a sated mind. When life draws to a close, former pleasures become dull and joyless functions, food brings no pleasure, desires vanish. That is why, from time immemorial, those who possess wealth have thirsted for immortality.

The meaning and purpose of eternal life depend, of course, on the nature of the mind that grasps it. Those without imagination will not know how to use unlimited time, and only one who has grasped the nature of enjoyment itself through yoga can savor the magnificence of the world in an eternal body.

India had always been a land of treasures and ancient knowledge, but two very particular secrets drew conquerors first: the secret of inexhaustible alchemical gold and the secret of eternal life. The age of the old gods ended after Sri Krishna closed the Vedic era, ceasing the worship of the ancient gods, Brahma the Creator and Indra the king of all the devatas. They were pensioned off, and the very fact that the millennia-long worship of these gods ceased gave invaders the opportunity to conquer India, though most of the raids were repelled. Seeing hordes of foreign barbarians, the immortals resolved to guard the secret of eternal life with the utmost care, and the task of "sealing" the secrets of the subcontinent was delegated to none other than Adi Shankaracharya.

A theologian and philosopher of the eighth century, Shankaracharya systematized the principal currents of Hindu philosophical schools, giving them the form in which they are known today. To unite the various sects, four mahavakyas — "four great sayings of the Upanishads" — were laid down for each of the four philosophical streams. Shankaracharya played an important role both in preserving ancient knowledge and in "sealing" Indian society. It was under his direction that, between 509 and 477 BCE, four abbeys were founded in four parts of the Indian subcontinent. Badrikashram Jyoti Pith was honored to hold the light of the Atharva Veda, Shardha Pith in Dwaraka preserved the essence of the Sama Veda, Govardhan Pith in Puri carried the wisdom of the Rig Veda, and Sringeri Sharada Pitham in the south became the cradle of the Yajur Veda.

Shankaracharya also "placed locks" on all twelve jyotirlingams. Pilgrimages continued, but the significance and importance of each holy site became blurred and unclear. The essence of the ancient secrets had not been open to all before, but even educated Brahmins lost the understanding of what this knowledge meant. Instead they kept clinging to the prescribed rituals and mantras. Yes, the secrets had been preserved, but forgotten; only their echo, distorted in the tunnel of time, reached the twenty-first century.

What exactly did Shankaracharya do, and were there any peculiarities to this "sealing of the secrets"? As Hanuman explained, Shankaracharya installed yantras of prosperity in the most important places of pilgrimage. More precisely, Sri Yantras. Some were found as bas-reliefs on temple walls, others were buried in specific spots or simply drawn in the sand. But all of them were set up and depicted inverted, overturned, which slowed development, induced a hibernation of mind, will, and progress. That, evidently, is why India looked frozen in time, dozing, indifferent to any effort not directed at daily survival.

The conquerors who burst upon Bharata unleashed monstrous calamities on the country, temples were desecrated, and ancient wisdom and science were eclipsed by the shadows of wars, conquests, and destruction. And yet the seals laid down by Shankaracharya preserved the sacred secrets, and the mleccha barbarians could not gain access to them. Alchemy continued to live in the shadow of Ayurveda. To this day, even in the most remote villages of India, people have some general notion of the power of mercury to create gold. Some wandering sadhus know of the golden pills of immortality made from processed mercury and capable of granting kaya kalpa,[footnote: Kaya kalpa — an intensive practice of purification, an ancient system of rejuvenation. Originating in India around 3000 BCE, kaya kalpa cleanses and nourishes body, mind, and soul through therapy with herbs and minerals, vital breathing practices, dietary guidance; it leads to the rebirth and rejuvenation of the organism and its stabilization in the state thus reached.] an eternally youthful body, and they even know how to make them. Whether these remedies actually work or not is another question, but the fact remains: this knowledge is the shadow of a greater secret that continues to live.

Around 1857, after the disintegration of the Mughal Empire, Mahavatar Babaji initiated Lahiri Mahasaya into the knowledge of Kriya Yoga. Until then, only myths and legends had spoken of immortals and their dealings with humanity. Babaji is the first immortal whose existence became publicly known, and whose life testifies that eternal life is possible.

The yoga granted by Mahavatar changed mankind. Becoming accessible to all strata of society, it hastened the arrival of the fifth yuga, which began to unfold from 1944 of the twentieth century.

Absorbing all of this information was not easy, but such was the essence of the history Hanuman recounted. And then He said something altogether unexpected: "The time has come to undo Shankaracharya's spell and free the sacred knowledge." Hanuman was entrusting this task to him, explaining that only a person who lives on a smashan could carry such a charge. All this sounded utterly unreal, yet he agreed. And could one refuse in such circumstances? He only asked, "How?" — and received detailed instructions; the rituals and techniques required enormous yogic concentration and physical strength. So began his string of journeys across India. On the first trip he visited Jageshwar in the Himalayas, and then, one by one, he went to all twelve jyotirlingams and the places where Shankaracharya's "seals" awaited their hour.

Badrikashram Jyoti Pith in the north, Shardha Pith of Dwaraka in the west, Govardhan Pith in Puri in the east, and Kanchi Kamkoti in the south… Only a few companions accompanying him knew the true aims of these journeys. Between all these trips there were intervals: he had to return to Govardhan to restore his strength and rebalance his sadhana. Recharging was important, since after performing rituals that demanded the utmost concentration he felt drained.

The last "unsealing" took place in 1998. Why did these "unlockings" have to happen at all, why did it matter? The true meaning of democracy was becoming clear even to those who had never dreamt of civic rights and democratic freedoms; societies were waking up. These political, economic, and cultural changes began after his journeys, however ambiguous or contrived that may sound. India was once again entering a path of prosperity in many respects. Yoga itself became extraordinarily popular throughout the world, spreading with the speed of a forest fire. Of course, the Indian peninsula concealed more riddles than the two Hanuman had mentioned. The very land on which he walked was steeped in primordial existential mysteries, and it was precisely because of these

mysteries that most rishis and yogis never left India, continuing to wander across it or to abide in deep meditations. With the lifting of the "seals," new seekers could gain access to ancient wisdom.

This story was not about deciphering ancient secrets or releasing dark forces, though there was something in it like the adventures of Harry Potter. Hanuman explained: as a result of these rituals, immortal beings would once again be able to appear to people from time to time. To inspire, to encourage the development of society, to give hope, to share knowledge of immortality.

People were already churning the ocean of digital, nuclear, and biochemical wonders and were determined to extract new, miraculous elixirs. Modern alchemy was being rediscovered; mortals were drawing close to uncovering the riddles of being. And yet they still needed guidance and hints. Immortality remained out of reach; even for the scientifically savvy modern person, the eternal life of the spirit in the casing of an indestructible body remained a tempting legend. People kept thinking and acting within the limited span of their lives; their ability to see the long-term perspective of history had been deliberately cut short so that the churning of the elixir of Time should remain unsolved. The nature of Time was still to be understood. To that end he taught others to awaken in themselves a vision of the future, to come out of the twilight of obscuration. Eternal life was to become not a curse but a reward for the contemporaries who dared to seek it. The golden bird — Bharata — was covering the ancient subcontinent, her eternal nest, with invisible wings, and the wind of change ruffled her feathers.

Journey to the North.
Meetings and Blessings

In May 1997, together with Umashankar Sharma and Rajendra Sharma, he set out from Govardhan, via Delhi, on a long automobile journey. The plains of northern India were already heating up, though the Himalayas were still draped in winter. The trip was hard, especially Kedarnath and Amarnath, and not only because of the weather but because of one mighty spirit caught in Kedarnath whom they were transporting to Amarnath to hand over to the Shiva Ganas. It was a fussy and burdensome business, the details not meant for disclosure, and no one would have dared to ask about it anyway.

The next ritual of "breaking Shankaracharya's seals" was to be performed in Badrinath, one of the four principal places of pilgrimage, the Char Dhams. It was believed that travelling there one could attain liberation, but their small group had a very different mission.

They arrived in the afternoon; Badrinath met them with a light snowfall. Because of the weather the normally busy town was almost empty. Umashankar Sharma, then working in the DDA, the Delhi Development Authority, managed to book them a VIP room in the hotel and, just in case, a bungalow as well. The bungalow was preferable, as it stood closer to the town center; leaving their luggage there, they set off for the temple. The views were stunning. The temple, set between the twin mountains "Nar" and "Narayan" and built by Shankaracharya himself, welcomed them with bright colors. Thanks to the intricately painted arches and columns, the heavy wooden doors and upper galleries, the architectural style resembled a Tibetan temple rather than anything else.

When they entered the temple, the snow had already turned into drizzle. There were at most a dozen visitors, and they had their fill of the powerful, peace-bestowing darshan of the Deity. They decided to attend morning service the next day. As they crossed the bridge that links the temple to the town, the rain grew heavier, and they stepped into the restaurant of the nearest hotel, which stood not far away at a crossroads.

Two elderly women appeared from nowhere and entered at the same time as they did; they had not been seen in the temple, and the town was utterly empty. Ordering tea, the women sat down at the next table. One of them, very tall and pale, with shoulder-length grey hair and a straight back, looked regal. She was dressed quite inappropriately for the weather, in a thin sari with a cotton blouse. Her companion was shorter, plumper, and just as lightly dressed. The women spoke with the accent characteristic of Gwalior, and Rajendra Sharma, passing by, asked: "Where are you from?" The shorter one replied: "From Shivpuri; my friend insisted that we set off for Badrinath at once, and that's how we got here." She pulled the trailing end of her sari more tightly around her shoulders, as if the thin cloth could warm her.

Rajendra came back: "They're from our parts. They seem to have neither money nor warm clothes…" They decided to help the two pilgrim grandmothers, treating them to a VIP reception and putting them up in the hotel, where they booked an extra room; they themselves already had the bungalow. The women were offered lodging, and they accepted with dignity. The disciples volunteered to escort the ladies to the hotel; as they were about to leave, he impulsively held out 250 rupees to the tall woman and said: "Amma, please bless me!" The expression on her face changed visibly, becoming serious, and she sank into the armchair next to him.

"A formal blessing is meaningless, but I shall create a Sankalp Ashirvad, a blessing filled with the resolve of fulfilment! Give me a coin, one rupee!" Clenching it in her right fist, she tucked it under her left armpit and grasped her right shoulder with her left hand. It was an unusual mudra; he had never encountered anything like it. Then she began to recite a mantra in Vedic Sanskrit, and it was an incredible recitation. Over many years he had heard various Vedic Sanskrit scholars and pandits, and not one could compare with this woman in pronunciation and diction. For about ten minutes they listened with reverence to the flow of Sanskrit shlokas, then she blessed each of them, one by one. To Umashankar, who had certain legal troubles, she said: "Very soon your difficulties will be resolved" — and so it happened, just as the woman foretold. To Rajendra, who was preparing to remarry, she said, "You will enjoy family life," and his marriage proved happy. Looking at him intently, she pronounced: "You will become world-famous. Like Hanuman" — and, still seated in the armchair, lifted both her feet: "Now bow before me!" All three obeyed, bowing low before her.

…The next morning the temple was overflowing. Luckily, they had reserved VIP seats the evening before and could now sit beside the altar in a specially designated place. Crowds of the faithful kept arriving; the drapery — shringar — covering the murti was removed so that everyone might behold the meter-high black stone, the Shaligram, depicting Lord Vishnu in the aspect of Sri Badrinath seated in padmasana.

One of the eight self-manifested images of Vishnu, the statue was found by Adi Shankaracharya in the river Alaknanda. The unusual idol was depicted seated in meditation rather than carved in the half-bent standing pose typical of canonical statues of Vishnu. The features of the face were almost entirely effaced by the flow of the river and time. It is well known that faceless idols are inauspicious — and Shankaracharya ordered the find thrown back into the river, then tried his luck again. But the same statue kept reappearing again and again, and at last it was placed in one of the caves near Narada Kund, and then installed in the temple. The Skanda Purana says that this idol was retrieved from a pond, not a river, but that hardly mattered any longer.

He was not ready for the keen, dagger-sharp instant recognition: the statue depicted Babaji. Imprinted in stone, Mahavatar looked at him from under half-effaced stone eyelids, and that gaze was riveting and intent. In this temple it was Babaji, not Vishnu, who was worshipped — he was convinced of it, recognizing the powerful and palpably heavy energy radiating from the stone image. The same stream of energy had struck him at his first meeting with the Immortal Guru in the flesh. The sound of a bell broke his concentration; the chief priest had begun the puja.

…Although Badrinath is in northern India, the chief priests, or Rawals, are traditionally Nambudiri Brahmins from the southern state of Kerala. The Nambudiri tradition was begun by Adi Shankara, and Badrinath is one of the few temples in the north where the ancient Tantra-Vidhi Yuga is followed. The Rawals are usually chosen by the former rulers of Garhwal and Travancore; they are accorded high status by the state governments of Uttarakhand and Uttar Pradesh and are revered by the royal family of Nepal. Given all of the above, the incident that followed was more than strange.

The priest who conducted the ritual singled out their small group and immediately took a clear dislike to him, openly displaying sudden hostility. Perhaps the muscular, broad-shouldered yogi with long hair and rudraksha malas struck the rawal as menacing, or perhaps the causes were other and deeper. In any case, after the puja the chief priest customarily received visitors, and they made their way to the Rawal's chamber, bringing with them a canister of water from the Yamuna as an offering to Badrinath. When they came in, certain high-ranking visitors from Maharashtra and Nagpur were already in the room — members of parliament and other important guests — and the rawal was enjoying the attention, full of his own importance.

Introducing himself and his companions, he told the priest that they had come from Govardhan and would like to offer the sacred Yamuna water to the Lord of Badrinath — and handed the small canister to the rawal. With a haughty smirk the priest exclaimed: "I shall drink it myself" — and, before the eyes of all, drained the water meant for the deity in long swallows… Flushed and gripped by anger, he rose, restraining a cane, and the atmosphere in the room rang with tension. "Well then, do you want to beat me with your stick? Go on, hit me!" And the rawal began to prance, ducking into another small chamber and popping back out, waltzing closer to him and then away again.

"Whack me here!" The priest bent over, insistently pointing at his buttocks. Realizing the absurdity of the scene, and thinking that it would calm a man who was clearly mentally unbalanced, he symbolically tapped him with the stick.

"Hit harder! Properly!" — the rawal was now grimacing, and that infuriated him. He struck the chief priest of the Badrinath temple lower down with the stick six or seven times, and the blows were hard. The rawal straightened up, squealing: "Thank you, thank you very much" — and ducked into the next room. The stunned bystanders sat in dead silence until the pujari assigned to accompany their group began to nudge them toward the exit, whispering nervously: "You must leave at once; he's a very important man and may call the police, complain — and you'll be arrested…" But nothing of the kind happened; Umashankar managed to get their car out of the parking lot ahead of the queue and they retreated. What was that? He was sure: the show was organized by Babaji. It was certainly an example of the Guru's dry humor: Babaji could test people, drawing out the best and the worst in them, tearing off pseudo-spiritual masks. Who else could make the chief pandit behave so arrogantly and defiantly, throw down a challenge to an unknown yogi, get a beating, and then thank him for the punishment? There was a presumed moral to the story: a swollen ego led to madness. High-ranking priests were merely rag dolls in the hands of immortals with a very specific sense of humor — they could turn any established reputation into a shameful grotesque.

life_events.event: Sharma set out from Govardhan with Umashankar Sharma and Rajendra Sharma on a long automobile journey to the north to perform the unsealing ritual at Badrinath.
They first transported a powerful spirit caught in Kedarnath to Amarnath to hand it over to the Shiva Ganas.

At a Badrinath restaurant Sharma met a regal elderly woman; he asked for her blessing and she performed a Sankalp Ashirvad in Vedic Sanskrit, foretelling that Umashankar's legal troubles would resolve, Rajendra's marriage would be happy, and Sharma would become world-famous like Hanuman; she lifted her feet and instructed them to bow.
At the Badrinath temple Sharma instantly recognized the Shaligram murti as the face of Babaji, not Vishnu.

After puja, the chief Rawal of Badrinath behaved arrogantly, drank Sharma's Yamuna offering, mocked him, and goaded him into beating him with a stick; Sharma struck him six or seven times below the back; the priest then thanked him.

results.english:
Over the years the story became a local legend and grew new details. He even once heard a version according to which ten men had been unable to restrain the wrathful yogi from Govardhan, the very one who had flogged the dancing rawal. After his memorable performance the priest did not live long. A few months later, after the temple closed for the winter season, he died of a heart attack in Rishikesh.

…Driving past the dharamshala, they noticed that the window of the room where the elderly women had stayed was wide open. Umashankar went to inquire whether the guests were still in the room, and as it turned out the manager was also looking for them. The door was locked, but no one was in the room, and the beds remained untouched, as though no one had slept in them.

Trying to take in a wider spectrum of signs and hidden connections, he never wrote off even small incidents as ordinary or accidental. And he preferred to think that Adi Shakti Herself had come to bless them, in the form of a tall, elderly woman of regal bearing who had not looked at all chilled in her thin cotton sari, unlike them, shivering in the piercing wind that spring in Badrinath.

Journey to the South

As part of the "Golden Bird" project (as he sometimes called it to himself), he made several journeys to specific places and temples in southern India, "opening the locks." The rituals performed had nothing of sorcery about them, yet had to be carried out with the utmost concentration and diligence.

The first trip in the spring of '97 was rather short: together with a medium, whose presence was required more for logistical arrangements than for the use of his particular "skills," they flew to Chennai to visit the monastery founded by Shankaracharya at Kanchipuram, the Kamakoti Pitam. From there they went to Sriperumbudur, where six years earlier Rajiv Gandhi had been killed in an assassination, along with fourteen other people.

The next stop was the hill town of Tirumala. There, by ancient tradition, before entering the temple people offer their hair to Tirupati Balaji (a form of Vishnu), who is believed to have come to save mankind from the woes of the Kali yuga. Devotees are promised a substantial reward if they shave their hair; for some pilgrims this is a symbolic gesture of repentance, purification, parting with old karma. He himself had no intention of parting with either his karmas or his long, jet-black hair, which contrasted sharply with the shaven heads of devotees. The heads of women, stripped of their lush long hair, looked especially bare and vulnerable…

The trip set the stage for the next journey, and in October 1997, accompanied by Devahuti and two disciples, Amit and his wife Shashi, he boarded the train from Delhi to Chennai. For all of them it was the longest railway journey. In Chennai they rested for a few days at the home of Mr. Pawar, a friend, disciple, and head of a well-known local television company, and then the train took them on to Rameshwaram. The narrow-gauge rails on the Pamban bridge could hardly be seen out of the windows; it seemed the train was sailing across water, and for several kilometers around there was nothing but ocean.

That very first evening they went to the beach, to the starting point of the famous Adam's Bridge,[footnote: Adam's Bridge — Rama's Bridge, or Rama Setu, is a chain of natural limestone formations between Rameshwaram (Pamban) Island off the coast of Tamil Nadu, India, and Mannar Island, Sri Lanka. This 48-kilometer chain is geological evidence of a former land connection between India and Sri Lanka. A mountain in Sri Lanka, Adam's Peak, is mentioned in several early Islamic sources as the place where the biblical Adam fell to earth from the Garden of Eden and then crossed the bridge to India. Some sections of the "bridge" are visible; the sea level in these places rarely exceeds 1 meter (3 feet) in depth. In the fifteenth century it could be crossed on foot until storms deepened the seabed. The records of the ancient temple of Ramanathaswamy say that Adam's Bridge stood entirely above sea level until a powerful cyclone destroyed it in 1480. Some archaeologists maintain that the stones on the surface of the sand are in fact older than the sand itself.] which links the Indian subcontinent with Sri Lanka. Twilight was thickening swiftly, high tides were covering the last barely visible stones of the ridge. Suddenly he noticed that with each interval between rolling waves, black butterflies were flying straight up out of the ocean. No less than 17–22 centimeters across, velvety black, with golden wing-tips, they surrounded him, fluttering and lightly brushing his shoulders. There were more than sixty of them, and they resembled living iris blossoms. Hypnotized by the sight of identical black silhouettes bursting forth from the restless waves, he and his companions watched the dazzling show. Then the butterflies vanished into the rapidly darkening air.

It was time to head back to the hotel. On the way they asked a tea-seller, who was already packing away his clinking glasses and pots: "Are there many butterflies here in the daytime?"

"What butterflies?" The old man knew nothing. Of all assumptions and theories, the simplest was to take this small event as an auspicious sign.

…At the entrance to the temple grounds they were stopped by a Tamil priest: "All men must be bare from the waist up to enter the house of God." It was a southern custom, and they obediently removed their kurtas. Seeing the heap of heavy rudraksha malas on his chest, the pandit asked him: "Are you from Nepal?"

"No, from Govardhan. Why do you ask?"

"So many rudrakshas…"

The twenty-two sacred wells within the vast temple complex were tied to stories of the Ramayana. Some of the pools had been named after the monkey-generals of Hanuman's army, and he liked that very much. Monkeys or not, the heroes were heroes, and their names had been kept for centuries.

Most pilgrims went down to the ocean in front of the temple for ablutions; then, in the temple, a bucket of water from each of the twenty-two wells was poured over them. This ceremony was believed to wash away sin. In his case, the ablution was obligatory before the ritual he had to perform. They came out to the ocean shore. Countless pilgrims were offering prayers for their departed ancestors, performing ablutions, loudly reciting or quietly whispering sacred words. Taking handfuls of damp sand, an old woman shaped something like a small lingam and offered it to the sea with a prayer. He caught hundreds of thoughts full of hopes, expectations, and pure faith. Wading toward the water, he stepped over old clothes shed by people, mixed with black, clayey sand: they symbolized old sins which, one hoped, had already been forgiven.

…Wet from head to foot, he stood in the temple courtyard while icy water was poured over him; his body went numb, yet the ritual was worth it: numbness gave way to a sharp sense of completion of a particular stage. This was not an ordinary ablution but a kind of penance; mixed together, water from different wells had a particular effect, and the impact of the rite was so strong that after the trial he slept for two hours.

In Rameshwaram the presence of Rama, the seventh and one of the most powerful avatars of Vishnu, was felt very strongly, as was the presence of Hanuman, who had sent him on the journey. Here, in this place, the history, rituals, faith, and intentions of gods and mortals fused together into a complex mosaic of Life; stretched out under the eternal sky, in plain sight of everyone, this fabric of being was open only to a few.

The next day a taxi took them to the southernmost point of the Indian peninsula, Kanyakumari. Having dropped anchor between the equator and the Tropic of Cancer, the gigantic ancient ship of the continent thrust into the ocean, and the incredible panorama was lit by a sunset that poured liquid red gold of the sun into the merging waters of the Indian Ocean, the Arabian Sea, and the Bay of Bengal; the salt breeze mingled with the breath of the earth, as if these two great beings could not have their fill of each other, and dusk was already beginning to hide their lasting embrace.

In the temple of Kumari Amman there were few people. Visitors walked around the inner altar, bringing offerings to the Goddess — traditional red saris and ghee lamps — repeating the thousand names of the Goddess.

…Having finished the ritual, he sat motionless, sensing the striking contrast between the intense vibrations of the North, its meditative melodies of Rajasthani ragas and the piercing bhajans still echoing in his subtle body, and the honeyed, syrupy quality of the lullaby that the South had been singing for millennia. The vibrations were soothing.

…The Meenakshi temple in Madurai struck them with its beauty and peaceful, powerful energies. The building had been raised on the very spot where Vishnu had given his sister and bride Meenakshi in marriage to Shiva.

Then there was Rameshwaram, the temple of Mallikarjuna in Srisailam, and Hyderabad… He meditated for long stretches in all these places; the rituals required concentration, visualization, and sometimes a silent invocation, and all this had to be done in a quiet place. Somehow, he always managed to find solitude in the spacious courtyards of the temple complexes. In the south the sight of meditating yogis in temples is not rare, and yet he often drew attention. Broad shoulders, long hair, and rudraksha malas drew visitors, although no one ventured to come near him during the rituals. But along the way, whenever they stopped for a bite, a small crowd would quickly gather around them. People thought he was a television actor, a Bollywood celebrity. Their attention was unobtrusive; southerners were polite, well-bred, and full of sincere devotion, as he could see for himself.

…Srikalahasti, the temple city in Andhra Pradesh, was not on their itinerary, but Mr. Pawar persuaded them to make the detour, since the place of the samadhi of Bharadwaj Rishi was not far off. He agreed, since paying respects to the great forefather and founder of their clan seemed obligatory. The place lay next to one of the temples representing the five elements, the Pancha Bhuta Sthalams.[footnote: Pancha Bhuta Sthalams — the five temples dedicated to Shiva. Pancha means "5", "bhuta" means "elements," and "sthala" means "place." Each temple represents a manifestation of one of the five elements — earth, water, fire, air, and ether. It is believed they are embodied in the five lingams installed in these temples, with each lingam named after the element it represents. All five temples are located on the same longitude, with only minor deviations.]

The famous Srikalahasti temple turned out to be stunningly beautiful. Dedicated to Shiva in His aspect of air, it is called "the southern Kashi" and "the Kailash of the South," and the seven-storied gateway tower is decorated with intricately carved images of celestial beings. At first the long queue dispirited them, but a temple trustee they knew led them inside through a VIP entrance. Passing through the famous colonnaded corridor, they came to the main sanctuary. There were two Shivalingams: one of stone, with images of a spider, a cobra, and an elephant, which it was forbidden to touch. Only pandits belonging to the Bharadwaj gotra and properly initiated could perform the rites. There was also another, invisible Shivalingam, set up in a closed chamber and "consisting" of air. A draft, coming from nowhere, swayed the flames of the oil lamps.

Here, standing on the invisible edge between the spiritual and the material, he felt the deepest

results.english:
symbolism of the element of air opening up to him. Bearer of Prana, of consciousness, of spirit, servant and consort of Life, powerful, infinite, and invisible like Time-Shiva, air symbolized both constant change and lasting permanence. The thoughts had no precise formulation — rather a state, the effect of the presence of higher forces…

The meditative state was suddenly broken. A middle-aged Tamil peasant who had been staring at him intently for some time suddenly cried out: "Har Har Mahadev!" and prostrated himself on the stone floor, touching his feet. To worship a mortal in the presence of a deity is utterly inappropriate, but the chain reaction had already begun, and the whole queue was instantly set in motion. People prostrated themselves before him, chanting praise to Shiva; the hands stretched out to him, the eyes filled with devotion and hope, made a staggering impression on him. What did these people see in him? Human faith, hope, and the desire to believe were all-pervading, like the air they all breathed.

Making Mother Happy

The benefit of any ritual always goes to the one who performs it. The participants may enjoy the spectacle and feel the force of the moment, but the blessing always goes to the shaktiman — the one with whom the grace of Shakti rests; the mantrin,[footnote: Mantrin is a Sanskrit word used to denote various government posts. In the Shaivite tradition, mantrin means "the one who recites a mantra."] who receives the boons of mantra recitation.

That was precisely how it worked, and that was one of the reasons why he kept beautifying the smashan and the areas adjoining it. Service to the land of Braj was a sacrament and an honor. Beyond the perfectly obvious rewards, the blessing of the land itself gave him colossal satisfaction.

Often, life in the immediate vicinity of the Divine Hill felt like a trial. One could remain an idle observer — or become an active participant in the endless Lila, the play of life. A shortage of money was never a lasting factor; funds always materialized, and the work continued.

It all began with the clearing of the neighboring pond, Radha Kund, whose surroundings were appallingly filthy. The whole area was strewn with rubbish, and had not been cleaned since the days of Krishna. One of the disciples spoke to the local administration, and the Siddha Siddhanta Academy was asked to "take patronage" of a 9-kilometer stretch of the parikrama road. No one else was going to do it. Thousands of pilgrims left mounds of garbage behind them every day, and the local people had cursed the city authorities for years: "Why won't anyone clean up this mess!" He and Devahuti hired a team of fifteen workers: "You will serve the Govardhan Hill, and we will pay you." All of them were provided with uniforms, given new jeans, red jackets, baseball caps, and shoes. "Now that you're dressed so stylishly, work in style!"

Over nine years that team accomplished an enormous amount of work, to the delight of the residents of Radha Kund. Every street and drainage ditch was cleaned, mountains of refuse were carted off, and even the Radha Kund smashan itself was transformed, perhaps for the first time in all its history. After nine years the government at last began to fulfill its obligations to provide amenities, allocating a hefty budget for new pavements and roads. But that did not mean there was nothing left to do.

One day the jungle behind the smashan caught fire. Whether it was carelessness or deliberate arson is hard to say, but the sight was truly heart-rending: nesting birds tried to dive back into the flames, to their nests, chicks and eggs. After the fire the trees stood scorched and wounded — a sight that was unbearable. The idea of planting a new plantation had been with him for a long time; the incident only strengthened his intention. Negotiations began with the state forest department: the Siddha Siddhanta Academy offered to look after the adjacent stretch of land. The negotiations dragged on for a long time, until a fateful intervention by an official from the Ministry of Labour brought about the desired result, and permission to plant trees on twenty-five acres of land was at last issued.

It so happened that at the same time a disciple from the USA sent him a generous donation of 21,000 dollars to buy a car. Thanking the sender, he decided to plant trees instead of buying a car. Besides, he never went anywhere anyway.

Clearing the area for the plantation began in October 2004, and it was a truly titanic battle with giant cacti and thorny bushes. Two huge JCB excavators worked for more than 700 hours, clearing the territory and leveling the soil. Trees were planted in the pits that had been dug, and water tanks were bought for irrigation. But even with regular watering the first batch did not take root — the soil was too acidic and too dry. Stubbornly, he planted again. This time the holes were filled with fertilizer, and the second attempt was a success. At first thin and fragile, the trees grew and gained strength, drawing underground water to the surface with their roots. Birds returned, wild antelopes were sighted, and during the monsoon peacocks danced among the trees. The plantation

results.english:
looked idyllic, but required constant care and funding. A sizable brigade of workers kept tending the area; keeping everyone on payroll was no easy matter. Yet those troubles brought him satisfaction.

Over the years he had learned to do everything only for the sake of obtaining satisfaction. That was the essence of any inspiration, like the feelings after a night-time sadhana, when the spirit is at peace and rejoices at life and at being in the moment. The same sense of all-embracing enjoyment of the general equilibrium and calm spurred him on to continue improving the landscape. All of this required money and effort, since he financed the clearing and irrigation of stretches of land that did not belong to him. This at first made the neighbors declare him an impractical spendthrift, and then watch his progress in astonished silence. Over the years bewilderment gave way to respect, just as silent as the initial skepticism. In the countryside (as everywhere) people are stingy with praise; only persistence and time can convince them of the nobility of another's intentions. And not always. All this work for the community so cemented his reputation that the local administration suggested he take care of the water bodies along the whole length of the parikrama. The water-supply project was handed over to the Siddha Siddhanta Yoga Academy, and he began to maintain the plumbing system and pay the electricity bills. Several cisterns were installed, and local residents at last gained access to drinking water; and somewhat later, drinking troughs for animals were set up along the parikrama road: stray cows and dogs, as well as the birds and monkeys of Braj, no longer suffered from thirst.

Supervising and maintaining the waterworks gave him exclusive knowledge of the local plumbing network, and he was able to provide an irrigation water supply for the plantation. The long-term plan for reforestation succeeded; the trees that had been planted survived several dry summers, and soon 50,000 fruit-bearing jojoba trees were already feeding all comers their berries. Kadamba trees, bushes, and thousands of bright, ever-blooming bougainvilleas were planted along the Mathura–Barsana road, as well as in the poorest neighborhoods of Govardhan. The greenery of the trees replaced the dry, desolate landscapes. And standing on the roof of his house and looking out at the ocean of lush greenery, he felt the long-awaited sense of deep satisfaction. The trees, survivors and triumphant, protected the wildlife and nourished the soil. Without a doubt, behind the success of all these efforts stood the Dark Lord of the Hill, Govardhan Maharaj himself.

…Local hospitals, cowsheds and farms, peasants without a stable water supply, came to him — and he gave water to those in need, since he remembered well his own first months on the smashan: a sun-scorched land without a drop of drinking water and the long, exhausting battle for survival…

However limited the budget, he always tried to help. To tell the truth, the budget was truly limitless, for there simply was none. Meanwhile, the town was growing; more and more policemen were being transferred to Govardhan. The old police station was rebuilt to house the new staff, but there was not enough space in the hostel, and locals were not eager to rent rooms to the newcomers. For all sorts of reasons. So the police captain came to him for help, since officers were living in unsuitable conditions, and the last thing the town needed was disgruntled policemen. On the donations of foreign disciples the Academy built a hostel for sixty people, equipped with bathrooms, showers, and water-treatment facilities. The completion of the building was marked by the installation of a stone plaque with the names of the sponsors, the Siddha Siddhanta Yoga Academy and Sri Shailendra Sharma.

During a flood, the lake Manasi Ganga next to the smashan overflowed, and water inundated the surrounding area within a radius of twenty to forty kilometers. The famous temple was submerged, as were many neighboring houses. Suddenly the local people began to praise him: "You are doing such great work; thank you for pumping out all that water…" There was no way back, although he had not yet even decided to take up the drainage. But the houses were flooded, property damaged, and he felt the waves of despair and hopelessness coming from people, the burning grief of homelessness. The discomfort of people affected the delicate balance of subtle energies of the area — and that reflected on his practice.

Large water pumps were rented, which proved a rather expensive undertaking. Foreign disciples again came to the rescue, and the pumps worked the water out without interruption. But that very water began to flood other farms, which required the installation of additional pumps. The success of the operation was nothing short of a miracle. In less than a month workers hired by the Academy were able to divert and drain a significant portion of the floodwaters. After previous floods it had taken the authorities about six months to carry out similar work. The happiness on people's faces was the greatest reward; the problem was resolved, and once again he experienced relief and satisfaction, since he was repaying the debt owed to the land that had received and supported him.

He once again became the talk of the town, this time without sneers and gossip. Respect had been earned by hard work. Now the Siddha Siddhanta Yoga Academy looked after 1.5 km of the Govardhan Hill, where irrigation pipes had been installed. They needed systematic maintenance, of course, but the watering system worked and the forest grew.

He kept on planting, and 150,000 trees took root along the roadside from the smashan to Radha Kund. It was an enormous task. Fortunately, funds kept coming. As did inspiration, from the most unexpected sources.

The idea of planting jamun fruit trees came from a monkey that had been living with them for nearly thirteen years, Chunmun the bandariya. One day someone brought jamun berries, and Chunmun ate them with such relish that he decided to plant berry trees for her relatives as well. No sooner said than done. They bought and planted more than 11,000 jamun trees, putting up by the new plantation a sign with a short but intriguing inscription: "11,000 jamun trees kindly donated by the monkey Chunmun to all kin." Chunmun became a celebrity overnight; one could only hope that her story would also inspire people.

Why did he bother with all this work at all? The expenses, the hassle of organizing workers and equipment, the tedium of dealing with bureaucrats… He did not earn a single rupee.

This place and the beings inhabiting it were entities with spiritual essences, in close interaction with his own. Sadhana had developed his sensitivity extraordinarily, and he knew: all animals, plants, stones, rivers, natural catastrophes and weather phenomena, sounds and words, were alive, interconnected, and conscious. A global understanding of existential equilibrium and the general interconnection of everything with everything had become a state of his soul.

results.english:
is always lived in relationships with other objects, places, and beings." Coming to know a world much vaster than the merely human, he could no longer rely on outdated postulates that social and cultural references could accurately describe reality. The world is far larger.

Anthropologists who had tried to classify such beliefs called them "animism." It is just another scholarly term, but at least it captured the idea of the absence of any distinction between the world of spirits and the physical world. At a subtle level these beliefs had rooted themselves in the very land; they only needed to be heard. Traditional, animistic, polytheistic, and partly shamanic religions had existed in pre-Vedic times as well. Being part of Hindu traditions, they are reflected in various Puranas. Long before Hinduism, Jainism, and Buddhism, people lived by animistic codes, and these beliefs were still alive and breathing through the stones, the trees, and the animals of Braj. Sometimes by the side of the road, under a tree, in a field, or on a narrow lane, stones lay that were routinely worshipped as Radha and Krishna, the Lord of the Govardhan Hill, or Hanuman. A pair of eyes would be drawn on a stone, a tilak of sindur paste applied, and the Deity was ready for worship and communion.

…As sadhana sharpened his sensitivity, he began to notice that the trees on the path to the Shiva temple were "trying" to touch him, leaning ever lower, stretching out their branches. Many "female" trees grew on the property, and they were clearly aware of his presence. When the house was still scarcely fit to live in, he used to sit outside, and once jokingly addressed an old neem tree: "Could you lend me a little more of your shade?" Over the next few months the tree turned its branches in the direction of his armchair, sheltering him from the merciless sun with a lush green canopy…

In Braj every tree is considered sacred. It is believed that for thousands of years ascetics have continued their austerities around the Govardhan Hill in the form of trees. Watching the miraculously surviving old trees, he was inclined to believe these legends. Some of them hardly seemed to be merely trees; to him they seemed to be living, breathing, meditating beings with weathered trunks and rough, cracked bark, their bodies mercilessly tested for resilience by extreme temperatures, winds, rains, insects, birds, and animals.

Every time he planted a new grove, he knew: new trees would take in souls that came here to continue their long transformative journey. And those trees, too, would develop further, taking on other forms, other bodies.

All these labors of love were rarely mentioned, only in passing. From time to time local newspapers wrote about the great work being done by the Siddha Siddhanta Yoga Academy, but journalists kept getting the facts wrong. Perhaps because they themselves regarded these stories as incredible. To spend private funds on the improvement of public lands was still considered unthinkable. But what he was doing was not the widespread religious charity of Braj, but something much greater — service to the Earth.

It is well known that human memory is short, and new generations of Govardhan residents would remember their native land as green; the sun-scorched fields and dry, barren hills would remain in the memory of their parents. They would think that there had always been a multitude of lush, blooming bushes and trees, fountains and water bodies along well-paved roads. From a famous Bollywood song he liked the line:

"Others will come tomorrow; they will speak more colourfully than I, there will be those who listen better than you. Why should anyone remember me?"

And the words of another song echoed in his mind:

"There will be no kings, no queens,
not even life itself,
but the dust of the earth
will carry the memory of us…"

People were busy with dreams, with the cares of the present, not with memories of the past. But the Earth had the longest memory. It softly whispered of eternal Life.

With the Rottweiler Jimmy, 2006

Spirits and All the Rest

Land of Phantoms

Long hours of meditation and vigorous bhastrikas gradually developed in him quite unusual abilities: he could discern the airy bodies of spirits, and of other, almost transparent, yet quite real, inhabitants of the astral realm. Dark and bright shadows slipped past the periphery of his vision, hiding behind or to the side, never appearing directly in front of him. At first it was even amusing, especially during visits to cremation grounds populated by various phantoms. Some of them had been people in previous lives, others had not… Over time he had become rather good at telling spirits apart, but then lost interest and did not bother to classify their origin. Similar abilities developed in some of the disciples as well, but they were more afraid of these new skills. To see was to believe — and what they saw was the bare underside of existence. Both sides of the coin of being were coated with a patina of struggle and desire. Sadhana sharpened sensitivity, and the new sensations and realizations were keen, sometimes frightening.

…When the period of the "battle for the farm" was over, the family moved again — to a rented house on the outskirts of Gwalior, where, near the closest local landmark, the Morar smashan, only a few residential houses stood. The place was quiet, with a large, well-tended garden, and in the afternoons he would usually head to the smashan for a walk with one of the visitors. Somehow conversations sounded deeper and more meaningful there. At the gates they were met by an Urdu inscription that melancholically reported: "Life brought us here — and we came; Death took us — and we go. We did not arrive of our own will, and we leave not of our own desire." It sounded believable.

Sometimes, during last rites, he watched terrified souls trying to prevent the attendants of the smashan from cremating their bodies. Some screamed soundlessly: "Don't burn me, I'm alive!" — trying to re-enter their "own" bodies and throwing themselves on the dead flesh they had inhabited only a moment ago. But they simply passed through, and no one heard their cries. As far as he could tell, the airy bodies of phantoms had almost the same shapes and sizes as their former bodily shells. Only one of them behaved with dignity — an army brigadier who had been honored with the highest military distinctions. Many high-ranking military men were present at his cremation; when the gun salute split the sky with sharp volleys, scaring up a flock of pigeons, the brigadier hesitated for only a moment, then looked at the mourners and, without trying to notify anyone of his presence, simply floated away, dissolving into the crowns of the trees. Perhaps among the shocked and homeless souls he was the most mature phantom.

…Encounters with other inhabitants of the smashan began as soon as he and his sister settled in Govardhan. They were strange and at times eerie beings. Catching sight of him, some fled in panic, others lingered for a few months, and some stayed forever.

After the dhuni was lit, new spirits began to appear. Drawn by the nighttime ritual, myriad translucent spheres emerged directly out of the flame, sometimes diving back into the dhuni or soaring above the Shivalingam in an ecstatic round dance. They never "belonged" to people and were not about to become so. The spirits of people kept to their own company. Here, on the ancient smashan, only the strongest could remain.

Since the repairs and clearing of the land continued for nearly three years, he simply could not afford the luxury of idly watching the spirits; they were not offended by his lack of attention. They were, rather, shocked and surprised by his ability to see them without showing it.

Several female spectres lived in a half-ruined building behind the house, and their translucent silhouettes, like weeping willows, could be seen at dusk. The Witches, as he nicknamed them, wore pointed wide-brimmed hats and long robes and recalled illustrations from children's books, but their blurred twenty-foot figures were far from playthings.

Quickly moving, iridescent translucent beings that lived in the large trees in front of the house seemed to be made of bundles of air. With curiosity they observed the new inhabitants of the smashan and their lives, but did not interfere — at least, not openly. It is hard to say to what class of being they belonged, but later he saw similar translucent forms in the Hollywood thriller "Predator." It seemed they were peaceful.

Five or six spirits had chosen a secluded spot beyond the pond, and would often sit on the stone wall, reciting mantras with rare religious fervor. They belonged to the Bengali line of Krishna devotees, drawn by the power of Moksha Bhumi, where they could attain final liberation. These spirits were "living examples" of how a connection with a genuine, ancient spiritual lineage ensured spiritual progress even after death. The phantoms of devotees did not seek new bodies, did not crave new births; they had accepted their status and were reconciled to their new form, wanting nothing other than peace and quiet.

Yet another peculiar phantom, a bhuta, lived in the house. More than 180 centimeters tall, in a cape of black feathers, with a thick long beak, he was like a giant raven, but under all those feathers he was humanlike. His thin, bony legs were turned inwards, his knees and feet pointing backwards, his heels facing forward. Moving with a peculiar dancing gait, in a strange rhythm, the phantom made sudden ducking movements. Whenever he caught sight of him, the raven-man "dived" aside, changing direction and avoiding direct collision. After their arrival at the smashan he stayed in the house for several months, apparently until December 1993.

And there were the spirits of a mother and daughter. The first, weeping desperately and moaning, would run through the house — a girl no older than ten. Rushing through the garden, she would throw herself into the pond, and the mother would follow her, calling out the name no one could ever make out. After some time the phantom of the woman vanished, but the spirit of the girl was seen for several more years — she followed her usual route until two skulls were found in the garden, one of a woman and one of a child. Perhaps both had died in an accident, or had been killed. In any case, they had fallen victims to some tragedy, and the belated rite of cremation gave their tortured souls peace.

Soon after this incident, while digging an irrigation channel by the cowsheds, workers found another eight or nine female skulls. Once more a quiet cremation rite was performed, guaranteeing the last peace. It is believed that the soul continues to remain near earthly remains until the last particle of organic tissue disintegrates, dissolves into the earth. The element of Earth is

results.english:
one of the five basic elements that make up the human body. The alchemy of creation is at once simple and complex: burnt bodies turn into ash, alchemical bhasma,[footnote: Bhasma — ash, or any powdery product of combustion.] and return to the earth as yet another constituent of the planet's eternal primordial matter. The body's ash is destined to become the dust of the earth.

…The house was often visited by one female phantom, a Churel, and the creature looked terrifying. Churels are the spirits of women who died with unfulfilled desires, usually loitering around graveyards, cremation grounds, abandoned battlefields, on house thresholds, and at crossroads. The old white house stood on an inner crossroads of the smashan, and perhaps that was why phantoms had chosen it as their "country club." Slowly drifting through the air, this Churel hovered, dangling from the ceiling fan, crawling across doors and shutters, clinging to them with her bony fingers. Bald, red-eyed, with black skin and large, fang-like yellow teeth, she dangled in the dark corners. The creature flaunted a horribly filthy blouse, torn at the front, out of which withered breasts spilled, and a tattered underskirt draping bony hips. Yes, and "she" also had a big mustache… Whoever her creator was, he had chosen the most repulsive palette. It was believed that anyone who saw a Churel would be struck with a fatal illness, and that whoever answered her nocturnal call would die. But this particular phantom was silent. He, Devahuti, and one of the disciples — who had given them newborn puppies — saw this charming creature. The Churel had become attached to the puppies and was constantly nearby; perhaps she was drawn by the smell of new life.

Once, when she was peeping at him from outside through the window, he jokingly called to her: "Hello, beautiful! How are you?" The phantom recoiled as if from a powerful blow and disappeared forever. Maybe some curse had been lifted, who knows. But one can say with certainty — that was the only compliment a Churel had ever been paid.

…The Land of liberation — Moksha Bhumi — was also the land of the Nagas. Huge serpents inhabited not only the old legends but also the garden, and one even once turned up in the darshan hall. The door stood open when a royal cobra slowly slithered in, almost 4.5 meters long and as thick as an anaconda, moving with graceful fluidity. Everyone froze; he quietly commanded: "Don't move…" The snake stopped, looked him in the eye, defecated on the carpet, and slid leisurely back outside. Perhaps that was how the Naga expressed its opinion about the noise made by the improvement works. The carpet had to be taken out and burned, since the possible effect of the venomous waste was unpredictable. A few days later, the long shed skin of the reptile, found by workers in the garden, was hung over the front door for the amusement of visitors. Snake charmers said the sign could be considered auspicious: if a royal cobra defecates in the house it means a blessing, as opposed to a bite. Venom versus dung. In those days he and his sister had had enough of both, mostly coming from the local people, so the presence of cobras was no objection; the snakes were noble and silent neighbors.

The God of Death

Although the land was officially in his ownership, de facto it belonged to the Lord of Death, Yama. Only He could allow anyone to live permanently on the holy Hill. Stretching from the royal smashan to the lake Kusum Sarovar, this stretch of the Hill had never been meant for habitation. Some ashrams had been built there, sadhus lived there, but no one stayed for good. At some point everyone had to move on, to other places or other worlds. In most cases, the residents were chosen by the Lord of the Hill himself, Govardhan Maharaj, and the God of Death, Yamaraj,[footnote: Yamaraj — also known as Dharmaraj, the lord of Dharma, the God of Death and justice in Hinduism.] sanctioned the candidacies. In the first week of November 1993 he received an unexpected official permission to remain on the smashan.

…He was dozing, half-reclining on the bed in the second-floor bedroom, when the enormous head of a black buffalo appeared in the window. A huge eye, like a wet black cobblestone, narrowed, and he froze. Hindus believe that to see a buffalo in a dream means a visit to Yamaraj. To put it bluntly, an imminent demise.

The buffalo suddenly sneezed, and the huge head swayed. Still unable to tell dream from trance, he found himself sitting on the back of the enormous animal. The hooves and horns of the black giant were painted gold, intricate gold ornaments adorned its huge neck. The ride was short; one did not feel that they were moving at all. And yet beneath himself he felt the movement of powerful muscles and the heat radiating from the huge, slightly damp body of the buffalo. His mind was empty; he felt not a drop of curiosity or nervousness.

The surreal journey was almost soothing, even if it might be his last. He found himself dismounting in pitch darkness. The place of arrival had no features; only the heavy breathing of the buffalo could be heard. Then he made out a faint glow and someone's silhouette. The stranger's gold ornaments glimmered in the darkness: a tiara, earrings, necklaces, bracelets on the arms and legs, a massive belt — and that was enough to make out the face.

Before him stood Yamaraj, the God of Death and of Dharma. Mighty, tall, and dark-skinned, the God was clothed only in a red dhoti; the palms of his long, mighty arms, with their magnificently outlined musculature, were bright red, as if painted, and his grim, serious face with sharp features was framed by a fiery red beard, without a moustache. There was neither mace nor noose, with which He usually catches the souls of the dead.

Bowing hurriedly before Yamaraj, he had a fraction of a second to admire the intricate gold ornament of the deity's sandals, woven from thin straps. Rising, he realized that he came only up to Yamaraj's waist — and then was pierced by a lightning flash of realization that they had met before and had known each other across past… deaths.

The God of Death spoke of the meaning of Moksha Bhumi, of the spirits inhabiting it, of the past and of the future. At some point the Lord of Dharma said something about Shiva, but he was too nervous to remember the exact words, catching only the general sense that Mahadev "would soon come to His consort."

…He was being permitted to remain on the smashan to look after the place — and that was the keynote of the meeting. There were no recollections of the return journey; perhaps there had been no journey at all: he simply found himself half-reclining on the bed in the bedroom. But in the tips of his fingers a distinct tactile memory of the hot, oily hide of the buffalo still lived, and his nostrils were filled with the bittersweet smell of the smoke he had breathed there in the dark. He was in no hurry to discuss what had happened with anyone but Devahuti.

There was no doubt that the smashan was in the hands of Yamaraj, whose giant buffalo often came down to the cowshed to drink. Probably, the animal had nowhere to quench its thirst in the realm of the God of Death. Puzzled cowherds complained that the cistern of water was once again mysteriously emptied — to tell them the real reason would have been unwise. It was already hard enough to find people willing to work at a cremation site, and stories like that would have scared off the most desperate.

The Trial of the Spirits and
the Tamers of Phantoms

It was Hanuman who first called him "Yogiraj." Apparently it was thus that the Deity had introduced him to the world of spirits, since all phantoms knew him by that name. Soon after his first communion with Hanuman various astral beings began appearing at the smashan, and all addressed him as "Lord of Yoga," Yogiraj. Usually this honorific is given to an advanced yogi, but it is also the title of the first guru of yoga, Lord Shiva. Some of the spirits called him "lord," "master," or "malik" if they were of Muslim origin — these beings were older than Islam itself. In any case, the way one is introduced matters in any society.

In northern India Hanuman is worshipped in the form of a child (Bala), called Balaji. According to belief, He tames evil spirits and possesses enormous strength (bal), which no demon can match. Full of compassion, Balaji-Hanuman also relieves pain, suffering, and misfortune. It is said that people possessed by benevolent spirits (dutas) are servants of Balaji, his messengers. They (possessed or "normal") are known as bhagats.

…The medium sat on the floor with closed eyes. Then, rhythmically lifting his hands above his head, he spoke quietly: "Balaji has ordered me to lead a darbar,[footnote: Darbar — a term, in Urdu (from Persian), meaning "court"; it can be the council of a feudal state for the conduct of affairs, or a ceremonial assembly.] at which people will be freed from evil spirits…" Appointed bhagat, the medium was to diagnose the nature and cause of each case of possession, exorcise the evil spirits, and heal the victims. And there was a particular condition stipulated by Balaji himself.

"Yogiraj, Balaji has commanded that your photograph stand in the darbar hall. And your name must be chanted aloud by all present."

Having thought it over, he agreed, and soon a large photograph of a young, broad-shouldered yogi with a red bandana on his head, looking straight into the camera without a smile, became the main accessory of the exorcism sessions. He did not remember who had taken this snapshot and when, but it seemed to work. After a successful darbar people chanted his name: Yogiraj Maharaj, ki jai!!! praising him and the grounding, liberating power of healing. There was no need to attend the darbars in person. The photographs and the jakara — the chanting of his name at the end of the session — were quite enough. The power of the yogic lineage, manifesting through him, helped everyone without exception. He did everything in his power to fulfill the new duties, and the "Spirit Rehabilitation Program" was operating successfully from December 1996
