results.english:
onward. By summer, the number of those possessed by spirits and healed had approached a thousand, but no one bothered to count the astral "clients."

He recalled the words of the great yogi Gorakhnath: "Meri bhakti, Guru ki Shakti" — "My devotion is reinforced by the power of the Guru," and made these words his personal slogan for difficult decisions and swift action. The world was severe and realistic, human bodies were fragile, and all souls needed support at various levels. Personal devotion and the power of the higher Guru helped maintain a balance between people and spirits. The Prem Darbar took place regularly three times a year, giving help to all without any discrimination: those suffering from possession and the spirits who had been wronged by people.

Any soul, regardless of whether it dwelt in a physical or airy form, could receive protection and ask for justice. To catch a phantom was not difficult; there were many exorcists who specialized in apprehending astral offenders. However, the task of the darbars was not only to detain a malevolent spirit and extract it from the body of the possessed, but also to "rectify" it, to help it find peace. That was the most important part of the work. People were of course helped too, but the "rehabilitation" of spirits was no less important.

For him personally this period proved rather difficult, not because of phantoms but because of the living. Some relatives were still critical of his way of life, and their dismissive attitude hurt. But when dozens of people who had received relief from suffering through his yogic abilities chanted his name, the stream of human gratitude and devotion stood in sharp contrast to philistine gossip and the condemnation of relatives.

As for the medium, things did not work out for him. Suppressed desires often wait for an opportunity to break out, to slake the thirst for money, power, sex. And the astrologer's son had no immunity against temptations, which in the end corrupted and devoured him. Balaji withdrew His grace. Let us say the medium was dismissed. He suddenly left town, and the darbars stopped. Several years passed, and in March 2009 a new visitor appeared at the smashan. This medium had been holding small trials over spirits on his own for many years. Long ago he had been given a book with yogic commentaries on the Gita, and he had long wanted to meet the author, but had kept putting off the visit. Until one day, during a ritual, a most powerful gust of wind burst into the courtyard where the darbar was being held and threw open the Bhagavad Gita (which the bhagat always brought with him to the proceedings), as if the book were being leafed through by impatient hands. When the wind died down, the participants of the ritual said that they had seen the astral form of a yogi whom, as it turned out, they all knew as "Guruji." The next day the medium went to Govardhan.

Bowing respectfully, the bhagat asked his permission to use his name and photograph during the "court sessions." He warned him of the dangers of abuse of authority and of the traps of the ego. Anyone dealing with astral law and order had to be filled with humility and modesty; this was the first rule of survival. Phantoms sniffed out human weaknesses splendidly and turned them into erupting volcanoes of desires and temptations that incinerated the weak in spirit.

…European disciples of the master, Rajkumar Sharma, were often introduced as a ghost-hunter, and everyone laughed willingly. But the medium's duties were more than serious. Over the next ten years, using the name of "Yogiraj," Rajkumar successfully summoned offending inhabitants of the astral world and decided their fates. There were other gatherings that conducted exorcism, but it was this darbar that became known as the most powerful and effective, thanks to its link with the yogi from the royal smashan.

Young-looking and trim, always dapperly dressed, with an orange tilak on his forehead matching his henna-dyed hair, Rajkumar did not lose his sense of humor even in the most difficult séances. Screaming women with bulging eyes and protruding tongues, the curses and threats spewed by the possessed, were for him only familiar sounds of the lower worlds. People shook, writhed, and uttered gibberish, but for Rajkumar these were not signs of schizophrenia or psychic deviation but a side effect of possession. Negotiations would begin with the inhabiting spirit, and a polite and calm discussion of the reasons for the "occupation" would in the end incline most "criminal" phantoms to cooperation. Some were "arrested" and punished until they agreed to abide by the rules.

Society regards exorcism as an archaic form of psychotherapy, since possession is a well-known archetypal phenomenon. The term itself is rarely mentioned in medical literature. Western medicine calls possession "dissociative identity disorder" or "multiple personality disorder." Such cases are usually placed in the category of medical anthropology, but for people in need of healing, the precision of terminology does not matter. In remote villages, doctors cannot diagnose the most seriously ill patients; proper psychoanalysis and psychopharmacology are rarely available there. Modern psychotherapy is the secular treatment of metaphorical "demons": psychological traumas, painful memories. Exorcism takes the existence of demons literally. Manifesting themselves as mental deviations, "evil spirits" are for bhagats and shamans real entities greedy for bodily experience.

As Rajkumar's ability to control spirits grew, his awe before the power of the Guru grew with it, since he repeatedly witnessed phantoms who, having beheld the astral image of the yogi, submitted, instantly recognizing the penetrating power of the dweller of the smashan. In the name of Yogiraj any spirit or phantom could be summoned. The cases varied. Sometimes they were the inventions of housewives disillusioned with family life or brides unwilling to marry, while at other times intervention required extreme caution.

Together with two other helpers and his wife, Rajkumar came to Govardhan regularly. There was no need to report to the Guru about current affairs in person, but the bhagat wanted to see the man whose power was again and again confirmed by the testimony of the penitent spirits. And this gave the ghost-hunter strength, since his occupation was, after all, rather dangerous.

From time to time the darbar organizers sent "patients" to the famous temple of Mehandipur, where in one of the halls a stone image of Balaji-Hanuman had been installed, from whose chest sacred water flowed whose source was unknown. It was believed that water mixed with the ash from the never-ending fire rite cured possession. No one could remain near the altar of Balaji — neither in human nor in astral form — but people with sufficient sensitivity sometimes asked Rajkumar about someone's airy body that they had noticed near Balaji. Those who had attended the darbars before instantly recognized the familiar astral form of Yogiraj. He himself never commented on these stories; he simply listened attentively to Rajkumar, sometimes instructing the bhagat in private.

Life went on as usual; seasonal harvest festivals alternated with religious holidays, gossip about political and other scandals gave way to rumors of new cases of possession. The twentieth year of the twenty-first century was approaching, and many spirits reported that soon the problems of the astral world would worsen — and would affect the world of men. The interrogated phantoms did not specify what kind of problems were to be expected. Some muttered something incoherent about a new world order, but once or twice the spirits whispered a long-forgotten name — "Mahamari."[footnote: Mahamari — Sanskrit/Hindi for a great pestilence or pandemic.]

Mehandipur

Some foreign disciples simply ignored discussions of phantoms and spirits; the pragmatic mind brushed off everything that had not been confirmed by Google and Wikipedia, or had no connection with yoga. It was hard for them to digest snatches and fragments of stories about black magic and the implantation of entities. Yet after several personal experiences, skepticism gave way to bewilderment and confusion, for the powers they encountered had no analog. The mental database of the foreigners consisted of well-grounded logical conclusions and incontrovertible scientific facts, and only after a personal collision with the paranormal did some accept the existence of a parallel reality. The other "agnostics" simply tried to ignore the topic. It was part of the Guru's duties to direct students toward new discoveries; more than once he repeated: "If you walk the spiritual path, you need to know the world of spirits as well."

The phantom of a seven-year-old child who had died in a car accident in a faraway country many years before, invisible but still suffering and lost, quietly roamed the apartment until he was "locked" into a coconut and brought to India. After a particular ritual the boy's soul received liberation, comfort, and the necessary guidance, which neither the prayers of the long-departed grandmother nor the apartment's renovation had been able to provide. There were enough such cases, but they were never discussed publicly. As a Guru of yoga, he did not officially specialize in taming phantoms, although he was well acquainted with all the details of the process.

In February 2008 two Russian disciples asked his permission to visit the famous "temple of phantoms" in Mehandipur. Their perception of India rested on a thick mixture of yogic texts and legends about mystical sadhus and avatars; now they wanted to see the rites of exorcism, and he did not object. Every serious seeker needs to come into contact with another reality in order to get an idea of the infinity of life. Astral forces are just as real as illness, madness, and inexplicable life tragedies.

The famous psychoanalyst Carl Jung once remarked: "What you resist persists." Beliefs in demons and evil spirits, who once populated the landscapes of human cultures, are written off as "primitive" traditions. Modernity labels possession by spirits as mental deviation. And yet, dwelling on the periphery of the human unconscious, some spirits still gladly use the world of people as their hunting grounds.

In exchange for the loss of medicine men and sorcerers, the psyches of Western inhabitants are populated by archetypes recognizable only linguistically. And yet the suppressed energies of the unconscious have remained. Wild, indescribable in words. In India, the denial of inner darkness manifested itself in ways long forgotten in the Western hemisphere. And in certain particular places these energies could be localized, tamed, and even "healed." And Mehandipur was the fortress that guarded the world of the living from the invasion of spirits.

…Setting out at dawn, from the car window they watched the countryside awakening. Heavily laden camel carts floated past; women defying gravity passed by in bright saris with bundles of brushwood and pitchers on their heads. Children in clean school uniforms or picturesque rags marched independently along the dusty roadside desert; thin dogs trotted by busily.

The bare and rather grim landscape was broken by occasional sun-scorched trees, whose thorny trunks darkened against the background of the Aravalli hills. The road into the outskirts of Mehandipur was clogged with cars, rattling buses, and ox carts. A thick veil of dust on the narrow main street draped the mishmash of people, cars, and mopeds. Their taxi slowly approached the massive temple, set between two hills, built of smoke-blackened stone with streaks of rain damage and copiously sprinkled with bird droppings. Some windows had been bricked up, and it was unclear whether this had been done to keep spirits from getting in, or to prevent them from getting out.

They got out of the car and were immediately surrounded by ragged children who began plucking at their sleeves: "Give ten rupees, sir, hello, hello!"

Entering the temple, they squinted, adjusting to the dim light. The echo of distant voices bounced off the walls; a torn cacophony of sounds poured in from all sides at once. In a passageway lay a woman on her side; her eyes were bulging, her hips moved in a furious rhythm, her loosened hair slid along the stone floor like living serpents. A man kneeling beside her was comforting her, smoothing the tangled hair; when she tried to bite his arm, her white teeth flashed. Stunned by what they saw, they pressed themselves against the wall, giving way to an entering procession — several men were carrying a large plywood board to which a young man was tied, his head rolling and bouncing on its surface, while deafening cries echoed down the corridors.

…The woman cleaved the crowd like a knife, as though her surroundings did not matter; her movements were measured and precise. Climbing to the top of a high staircase, she lay down on her stomach and slid down the stone steps. Lightly and elastically gliding, she flowed downward as if boneless, slipping between people like an eel, and no

one tried to catch her. Standing up, the woman climbed the steps again, as though catching the next wave. As she passed by, a stray strand of long hair brushed the shoulder of one of them, sending an impulse of something dark and unknown. Whispering, "Mujhe chhor do!" ("Leave me alone!"), unaware of anything around her, she was caught in a looped, endless cycle of movement.

A bronze bell hanging from the low ceiling, blackened by ash, rang ceaselessly; pandits muttered mantras. In the dim, reddish light they stood before the altar of Balaji, and inside the enclosure attendants threw offerings into the fire. People's mouths were half-open as if they were trying to eat the sticky sweet smoke rising from the white-hot coals. A stone obelisk painted gold, with a monkey mouth beneath two black eyes, was the shrine — here Hanuman-Balaji appeared as an untamed, raw, unpredictable force.

The driver who accompanied them pointed at another hall: "Pret Raj Sarkar, Lord Hanuman's assistant, who punishes phantoms." They saw a large, irregular stone with piercing black eyes, whose flat surface was covered with silvery paint or foil and decorated with strange patterns of gold squares. Before the journey they had tried to read about the temple, but had been unable to find information about this deity. Pret Raj, the king of phantoms, was not mentioned in Wikipedia. The stone radiated a heavy, oppressive energy, and they quickly left the hall, trying not to step on the men and women sitting on the floor. Some were in trance, others in visible agitation and obviously not themselves — the place was definitely not a tourist spot. Rather, it was meant to awaken the sense of the supernatural. Here the healing from madness and despair took place, and this noble undertaking was at once dreadful and pitiful.

A thin man in a yellow-stained kurta squatted in a corner of the hall, surrounded by a group of several people. His head and shoulders shuddered, as though he were trying to shake off the clouds of surrounding smoke. One of the companions stroked his arm and spoke in a pleading tone, negotiating with the phantom that had taken hold of the emaciated body of the man.

Another stone peeped out from a heap of flower garlands, and black eyes were painted on it too. A small trident, smeared with sindur paste — the trishul of Shiva — had been set up beside it. The painted eyes stared unwaveringly at a round hole in the floor. Mahakal Bhairav, the "god of punishment," whose court was held here, had yet another strange title — Captain Kotwal. In a hissing whisper the driver tried to explain to them the origin of the deity's name, but he lacked the English words. All they could understand and surmise was that this aspect of Shiva is also the Kotwal, the general of Hanuman's army.

Meanwhile a group of men carried in a woman bound with chains, who was quickly suspended head down over the opening in the floor, and her loosened hair became a continuation of the darkness disappearing into the depths. The men began to pull and release the rope, as though dipping the woman's head into the abyss; she struck her forehead against the edge of the opening again and again, and her cries of anguish carried through the hall.

"That is her preta[footnote: Preta — a supernatural being, a hungry phantom. It is believed that pretas in their past lives were greedy, depraved, and cruel creatures.] speaking, not her — and he refuses to leave!" said the driver. The men seemed to be gently exhorting the spirit. At last, they lifted the woman high above the floor, and one of them took her into his arms, carefully rocking her. Her eyes had rolled back, she looked exhausted but peaceful.

In the courtyard they came upon several people chained to the temple wall. The space was enclosed by the rear façade on one side and an iron lattice fence on the other, and bore little difference from a prison. Finding themselves on an open terrace, they tried to catch their breath and recover from what they had seen. The floor was strewn with flower petals, dust, and brown lumps of crushed sweets. The respite was short. Nearby a middle-aged woman with a drawn-out howl collapsed to the ground, and two pandits laid her face down, pinning her ankles and shoulders to the floor. Another carefully placed a stone slab on her lower back and another stone on her buttocks, and began chanting softly. The woman's groans died down; her lips parted, her breathing grew hoarse but even.

They felt drained, exhausted; clothes clung to sweaty backs, their faces were damp with sweat. Clouds floated by, covering the sun, and the noon was full of shadows that shifted their outlines and meanings. From the terrace they could see vertical stone slabs on the hillside.

"Are those gravestones?"

"No, those are markers for abandoned spirits. Some buy such a stone, and then the preta must remain beneath it; it will no longer trouble these people," replied the driver, who looked notably tired and seemed irritated. The hillside was studded with these slabs, and ravens perched above the dwellings of the spirits, like phantoms in dark cloaks still looking out for elusive prey.

…They had been told to eat nothing and drink nothing in Mehandipur, and not to look back, and they followed all instructions. They returned to the car with a sense of relief; the whole long way back no one spoke a word. On returning, they went to the darshan hall to bow before the Guru. He did not seem impressed by their accounts; recounting the eerie atmosphere of the temple, the complete absence of any blessedness or peace so characteristic of Indian places of worship, both disciples agreed that the temple of Mehandipur resembled a huge transit point, a station where arriving spirits waited for the next carrier…

Suddenly the Guru suggested that one of them check the water level in the pond, right now. Surprised, he got up and went out, feeling broken and tired. Sitting on the stone steps by the pond, still warm from the sun, he felt himself losing consciousness, and at that very moment he felt a push below his left shoulder blade. Fighting nausea, he turned and saw a small dusty whirlwind from which a half-transparent figure of a boy emerged. The figure quickly scrambled up the steps and dashed for the gate. The disciple was too exhausted to chase him or to ask the guard why a child had been let onto the smashan. Returning to the darshan hall, he reported the incident to the Guru, who said: "Yes, you brought a visitor. Some spirits want to see me personally, and sometimes they are allowed to." He advised the disciple to return to the hotel and rest properly. Unable to think of anything in particular, with a buzzing head, the disciple lay sleepless for a long time, filled with an inexplicable sadness. In the end he fell into oblivion, but slept little and badly. In the morning he felt better, as though the unexpected melancholy that had briefly nested in his heart had flown back to Mehandipur, gliding above the misty winter fields of Braj.

Gaya Kshetra

The smashan offered unlimited opportunities to observe spirits, to learn about the stages of the "otherworldly" journey of the soul, and to grasp the laws of subsequent incarnations. Dangerous astral beings now bowed before a mortal yogi, recognizing the power that stood behind him. Hanuman had shared a particle of his power with him, and by 1998 they were on good terms. Contacts had become rather frequent; the tasks He gave were carried out successfully. For the most part these tasks were connected with the affairs of the world of phantoms; in some cases only a human being could resolve certain problems.

One day Hanuman appeared in the company of a silent spirit needing a liberation ritual. The phantom of a thirty-year-old beardless man was nothing remarkable in itself, but the case proved unusual. Once, this person had been the messiah of one of the largest world religions and had carried out a colossal mission of rethinking religious and cultural landscapes. His followers founded a whole institution of worship and then, with the help of a powerful ritual, bound all the sins committed by the flock to the name of the messiah. It came to be believed that sinners were automatically purified, and now only the prophet himself bore responsibility for their deeds. There was no end in sight to the suffering of this spirit; burdened by old and new crimes of mankind, he was stuck in the lower worlds.

The blurred facts of this messiah's life and the legends of his death continued to grease the wheels of various religious corporations, and that rotation was not supposed to stop. Liberation was out of the question; the spirit was a trademark feeding a ramified network of organizations, and his name was used for the consolidation of funds, property, power, and souls.

The body of the former messiah was not cremated but buried, with certain parts kept as relics. Though almost decayed, these tokens of his earthly existence prevented the spirit from going on to higher spheres. As long as the remains were preserved, he was doomed to remain bound to the earth. Moreover, he was "immobilized" by the heavy karmas of millions of the faithful, the majority of whom believed in Him not out of purity of heart but out of inertia, by inherited tradition. During his short life this person had done only good, and yet he suffered even after death, unable to shake off the bonds of "obligations." The love and devotion of the masses can be wearying, especially if your name is constantly used as a cure-all. It is a heavy burden and an excruciating existence.

The pale spirit gazed at him impassively and yet expectantly, and out of astonishment he could not speak for some time. But in the company of gods and spirits no conversation is required, since any thought is expressed mentally.

Who would believe that Hanuman is engaged in "post-mortem affairs" of spirits of other faiths? And then he understood — there, in the Light, there are no distinctions of rank or creed. He agreed to fulfill the request; could one refuse such beings? Suppressing his astonishment, he merely inquired about the technical details and received instructions, together with the exact time and place for the rituals, and warnings about the danger of such intervention. Akin to walking a tightrope between life and death, these rites required enormous concentration of attention and physical strength; lifting the spells laid upon him could turn out to be lethal, and the observance of certain precautions was mandatory. In August 1999, together with the disciple and friend Mr. Javalekar, who organized all the logistics, they set out for Bihar.

…The city of Gaya is surrounded by three well-known hills: Brahmayoni, Pret-shila, and Ram-shila, and some of them had to be visited. According to numerous legends, the Pancha Koshi Gaya Kshetra is considered the most holy place on earth — here the body of the demon Gayasura turned into the rocky hills that formed the landscape, which was believed to possess the power of purification. For millennia people had been coming here to perform rituals for the deceased, since the performance of the rite of shraddha was meant to dispel

the sins of the departed and grant their souls liberation and peace. In Gaya there live hereditary Brahmins belonging to the Gayawal clan, supposedly created by Brahma to assist in the fire sacrifice after the destruction of the demon Gayasura. The Creator made fourteen clans, or gotras, specifically to carry out the rites, and about a hundred families of these Brahmins still live in southern Gaya, on the banks of the Phalgu[footnote: Phalgu (Falgu), the river — the river that deceived none other than Rama's wife, Sita. During her exile, Rama and Sita decided to rest on the sandy banks of the river. When the time came for the offering of food to the ancestors, Rama went for provisions, and Sita remained to wait. Suddenly the spirit of Rama's father appeared and asked that the ceremony be performed without delay. Instead of waiting for the offerings, Sita offered him sand. Fearing that her husband would not believe her, she asked the sacrificial fire, a cow, the river, a passing Brahmin, and a tree to be witnesses to the ritual. When Rama returned and questioned the witnesses, they all lied, hoping for repeated offerings. All except the tree said that Sita was lying. The angered goddess cursed them all, except the tree. Thus the river was doomed to run underground, hiding in shame. To this day, for most of the year, the river shrinks to the size of a brook wandering across a wide sandy bed.] river and around the Vishnu temple. A branch of the Gayawal clan, known as the Dhami, is still responsible for the rituals performed in the five sacred sites of Gaya.

They contacted one of the Dhami pandits, and on 19 August, from early morning until late evening, performed the rituals. That day he had to fast and allowed himself only occasional sips of water.

About 8 kilometers northwest of Gaya, on the hill of Pret-shila, lies the place for the rite of repose of the souls of the departed, pind dana. The temple of the Lord of Spirits, Preta Raj, is on top of a steep hill; six hundred steps lead up to it. He insisted on the ascent, and the pandit accompanying them, who had never been to the top of the hill, agreed. The place turned out to be deserted, as the temple of Preta Raj is visited only for rituals for the deceased. The "Garuda Purana" says that the souls of ancestors come to Gaya Dhama to receive offerings from the clefts of the hill Pret-shila. Many people believe that spirits pass through the rocks, moving from one world to another, and their movements create strange whispering sounds. Shadows appear where they should not be, and evidence of the presence of spirits is best not sought at all, especially after sunset.

A large boulder, named in honor of the daughter of the Lord of Death, marked the place for the offerings. They walked around it, scattering a little flour made of various grains. The place was eerie. At noon there were only four people at the temple; a dark-skinned middle-aged man squatting against the wall stared at them intently and unwaveringly. As soon as the ritual began, a whistling sound of a strong wind rang out, resembling distant singing, as though a chorus of spirits were reciting some indistinct mantras. The air did not move, but the sound continued until the rite was over.

On a boulder nearby was another famous landmark — the footprint of Lord Brahma — but they only briefly glanced at the hollow in the stone; they had to hurry down the slope, to be in time for several more rites.

One of the rituals turned out to be quite peaceful, another extremely exhausting. At the well of the dead, Pret Bhut Baori, he had to use iron and fire while remaining in continuous concentration — a tiring and strange process. At the end, the coconut used as a vessel for invoking the spirit had to be thrown into the well, and the pandit had told him in advance not to look back, since the ritual would have to be started again. The well had something like a lid, opening from one side, and he simply pushed the coconut through the slit — and a desperate weeping, reflected by the walls of the well, pierced his ears with a dull, furious force. Hurrying away, he struggled against the overwhelming desire to look back, and the sound followed him for a long time.

By now they were all rather tired; the last stop awaited them on the other bank of the river Phalgu, at Sita Kund, where Sita Devi had once performed a memorial rite for her father-in-law. There they had to make an offering, bringing to the Goddess the fruit sitaphal, named after Sita herself. When it was all over, he stood on the steps leading from a small mandir to the dry riverbed, contemplating the irony of the divine play. To become truly free, astral beings had to come down to Earth and entrust themselves to mortals. In places such as Gaya Kshetra, the ancient forces were mighty and palpable everywhere, but truly knew about them only a few.

In the pandit's house an abundant supper was laid out for them: mountains of rice, towers of flatbread, bowls of dal, curry, various sweets. He stared in astonishment at the preparations for the feast.

"Who is going to eat all this?"

The pandit smiled: "If you can't, you'll leave it…" They began to eat and could not stop. He finished every crumb of forty chapatis, as if someone else were swallowing along with him… or through him.

After supper he had to fill out a special certificate in which the name of the deceased had to be entered, as each puja of this kind was registered in the archives. Reading the name, the priest was visibly shaken, but then he shared with them an unusual story. One Indian businessman from Germany had once come to Gaya to perform a similar ritual — none other than Hitler had begun insistently appearing in his dreams. The Führer demanded that a ritual of liberation be performed for him. Worn out by sleeplessness, the businessman set off for Bihar, where he commissioned a similar puja for the former leader of the Third Reich. The story sounded wild, but had they not just performed a rite for the spirit of one even more famous than Hitler?

Powerful phantoms could communicate their wishes directly, but they entered into contact with sorcerers or mediums of the Western hemisphere rather rarely. They preferred to carry out what was needed in India, where the efficacy of the ancient rites was still effective. That is precisely why spirits of all ranks kept coming to the smashan, to the yogi who had passed through many death-samadhis, who possessed mind, breath, heart, and impulses. Familiar with the transition from the world of the living into the abyss of nothingness, he could withstand the furious onslaught of the astral entities and forces that opposed him there.

…At the beginning of the trip, on arrival at the railway station of Gaya, he had stepped on the scales out of sheer curiosity. Before leaving, he decided to do the same. It turned out that in a single day he had lost 7.5 kilograms. It took quite a long time not only to regain the weight but also to recover from the mental aftereffects of the intense rites. Even years later he felt that the sacraments performed still exerted a certain effect on him, though he did not regret what had been done. He was, rather, glad that he had at least tried to help the soul of a person who had come to change people's hearts and along the way had changed the history of mankind. Now this spirit was liberated from all obligations.

To foresee the outcome of the ritual performed, or to explain its denouement logically, was impossible, and no one informed him of the results. He had simply solved the problem, that was all. To analyze this extraordinary case is in order, but he could not arrive at a conclusion about the long-term consequences of the ritual, although certain guesses he had.

As the world beyond the gates of the smashan continued to seethe, so did the fermentation of collective and individual karmas accelerate, for there were no longer prophets onto whom collective and personal sins could be offloaded. Or they kept silent. He himself preferred not to call the coming changes "Judgment Day," but to view them as a gradual transition to the "Era of Earthlings." As the awakening of the human mind accelerated, compassion was to gain the upper hand over cruelty, and personal responsibility was to grow in geometrical progression to the ambitions of people. This was by no means an idealistic utopia, but rather the law of future survival.

Brahmarakshasa

In 2006 the smashan was visited by a modest schoolteacher named Umashankar Sharma, a reserved Brahmin from Kanpur, who had somehow begun to commune with a particularly powerful astral being — and had become his medium. From that time the favor of the teacher was sought by many businessmen and politicians who looked for luck in investments or guarantees of victory in elections. Becoming famous, Umashankar remained inconspicuous and modest, and channeled generous donations toward the construction of a temple of Hanuman. He came to the smashan at the bidding of his astral "friend," to introduce himself.

The spirit spoke up as soon as Umashankar entered the darshan hall. The astral being turned out to be a Brahmarakshasa, the powerful phantom of a Brahmin who in his previous life had been called Chote Maharaj (young master). "Brahma" means a Brahmin, and Rakshasa is a demon. The phantoms of Brahmins are extraordinarily powerful; to fight them and to drive them out is hard, since they have knowledge of their previous lives and remember all the Vedic texts and the complex rituals. Some who were initiated into powerful mantras during life retain subtle ties with deities after death, and there is nothing more dangerous than an educated demon.

The story of the phantom was extraordinary. A Brahmin priest, Chote Maharaj lived in a remote village, looking after an ancient temple of Hanuman. Being devoted to the deity, he renounced family life and devoted all his time to the upkeep of the temple. An educated and very devout pandit, he did not take sannyasa but simply remained an unmarried brahmachari priest, following the strict discipline handed down by his guru. He was a very pious man.

The temple adjoined a vast tract of agricultural land, and it was precisely that land that became the cause of the priest's premature death. Several times influential landowners — the thakurs — tried to take possession of the land, but the pandit was unyielding. One day after the festival of Hanuman, he was returning alone from the temple home, to the edge of the village. Several bandits hired by the thakurs ambushed and killed him. The pandit had only twenty rupees in his pockets, but the killers did not take that money, since they had been generously rewarded by their employers for "services." After the death of Chote Maharaj the thakurs seized the temple land, but they did not enjoy the spoils for long.

The murdered priest became a powerful and vengeful spirit. Standing before the Lord of Death, Yamaraj, every Brahmin becomes a brahmapreta, a phantom; this is the usual result of the post-mortem "verdict." But if a Brahmin who had observed the strictest religious and spiritual discipline (as Chote Maharaj had) dies a violent death, as a result of premeditated murder,

Briefly viewed the footprint of Lord Brahma; descended to perform more rites.

relationships.evidence_quote_en: The Creator made fourteen clans, or gotras, specifically to carry out the rites.
The angered goddess cursed them all, except the tree. Thus the river was doomed to run underground.
Sita Devi had once performed a memorial rite for her father-in-law.
He was, rather, glad that he had at least tried to help the soul of a person who had come to change people's hearts.
Obsessed by a thirst for vengeance, Chote Maharaj killed twenty-seven men of the thakur clan.
I am satisfied with your tapas and pleased with your repentance, Brahmin.
They simply concluded a pact of cooperation.

teachings.teaching: To become truly free, astral beings must descend to Earth and entrust themselves to mortals; only here can the rites take effect.
teachings.lens_note: Why all the great pinda-danas are anchored at Gaya; the soil is the operative element.

teachings.teaching: A single day of Gaya ritual cost Sharma 7.5 kg of body weight; the price of one liberation is paid in the body of the one who liberates.
teachings.lens_note: Yogic accounting is concrete; the mantrin pays in flesh.

teachings.teaching: The era of prophets who absorb collective sin has ended; the coming 'Era of Earthlings' requires personal responsibility proportional to ambition.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma replaces 'Judgment Day' with a maturity model — compassion over cruelty, accountability geometric to power.

teachings.teaching: A Brahmin who dies a violent unavenged death after strict discipline becomes a brahmarakshasa: a demon retaining full mantric knowledge — the most dangerous and intelligent of phantoms, only persuaded by reason.
teachings.lens_note: Exorcism by exorcism is useless against them; only dialogue and Hanuman's grace work.
